title: Efficient Hamiltonian Engineering for Adiabatic MIS Algorithms
authors: Guy Karni, Noam Cohen, Adi Pick
abstract: We present a hybrid adiabatic algorithm for maximum independent set (MIS) using Rydberg atom arrays. We engineer local controls that preferentially excite atoms with few neighbors, which represent graph nodes with small degrees. Numerical simulations show that the designed pulses accelerate convergence to the MIS state and suppress population in trap states. We obtain higher success probabilities than traditional global controls and a $25\%$ reduction in fidelity decay rate as problem hardness increases.
summary: The paper introduces a new adiabatic quantum algorithm for the Maximum Independent Set problem using local, degree-dependent detuning in Rydberg atom arrays. By engineering the Hamiltonian to favor low-degree nodes, the method suppresses trap states and significantly improves the success probability and scaling compared to traditional global control methods.
main_problem: Improving the efficiency of Adiabatic Quantum Computation (AQC) for solving the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem by addressing the shrinking spectral gap and the presence of trap states.
main_result: The proposed Local Degree-dependent AQC (LD-AQC) outperforms traditional global controls, achieving higher success probabilities and a 25% reduction in the fidelity decay rate as problem hardness increases.
method: The authors implement a Hamiltonian engineering approach using degree-dependent local detuning profiles that preferentially excite atoms with low local degrees, combined with a classical preprocessing algorithm (Algorithm 0) to optimize detuning parameters.
model_or_system: The study uses Rydberg atom arrays (neutral atom arrays) where the MIS problem is mapped to the preparation of a state with maximal Rydberg excitations under a Hamiltonian with Rydberg-Rydberg interactions.
key_observables: Success probability (P_MIS), approximation ratio error, average infidelity, and the hardness parameter (HP_trad and HP_LD).
important_parameters: Local degree (d_i), detuning profiles (linear, exponential, power-law), protocol duration (t_f), and the energy difference function (D_k(a)).
main_assumptions: The existence of optimal parameters (a*) is assumed for general detuning functions; the Rydberg blockade interaction is assumed to be sufficiently strong (U > delta_0^2 * f_max) to prevent unwanted excitations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the energy spectrum lifting degeneracy in the IS band, benchmarking plots showing superior success probability and lower error for LD-AQC, and scaling analysis of the success probability metric against hardness parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the MIS problem in AQC, describes the LD-AQC Hamiltonian engineering method, presents a classical algorithm for parameter optimization, provides mathematical proofs for energy gap maintenance, and concludes with numerical simulations and scaling analysis.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in many-body dynamics and quantum control, as it demonstrates how spatially-dependent Hamiltonian engineering can be used to manipulate spectral gaps and suppress transitions into unwanted many-body states.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (a) King’s graph with vertices labeled by their degrees. Colored vertices highlight independent sets (IS) of size 3, 4, and 5. (b) Probability that a vertex belongs to the MIS versus degree. Numerical average over 10,000 King’s graphs (red) is compared with the analytic estimate (blue). (c) The LD-AQC algorithm uses linear detuning profiles, ∆i(t), with smaller degrees corresponding to larger slopes. (d-e) The LD-AQC algorithm (Alg. 0) requires finding a critical size (k∗) and detuning parameter (a∗). The former is found using the sorted degree table (d) while the latter is found using the eigenvalues of the parameterized Hamiltonian at the final time H(tf; a) [Eq. (1)] (e). The...

Fig. 2. (a) A representative graph marking the MIS (red) and two independent sets (IS) of size |MIS| −1: a connected IS, which is subset of the MIS (green), and a disconnected IS, which is not (yellow). The shaded nodes indicate vertices that are not common to all three sets. (b) Eigenvalue spec- trum of H [Eq. (1)] at tf, sorted by the number of Rydberg excitations. The degenerate IS band in the traditional AQC is shaded in gray, while the non-degenerate IS band in the LD-AQC is shaded in yellow. Connected and disconnected IS states are denoted by green and orange circles respectively.

Fig. 3. (a) Benchmarking performance of the traditional and LD-AQC algorithms. Plotting the average success probability PMIS = |⟨ψMIS|ψ(tf)⟩|2 (right axis) and approximation ratio error 1 −R with R ≡⟨ψ(tf)|ˆn|ψ(tf)⟩(left axis) as a function of protocol duration tf, averaged over hundreds of instances of 11-node MIS problems. (b) Success probability metric, SPM ≡−log(1 −PMIS), evaluated against the hardness pa- rameter HP for the graph ensemble from (a). (c) The SPM of LD-AQC vs. the traditional algorithm, for the data from (a,b).

Fig. 4. (a) Average infidelity ⟨1 −PMIS⟩versus total protocol time for specific hardest graphs subset (2.2 < HP ≤4.0). The traditional homogeneous AQC (purple) is compared against local detuning utilizing exponential (green) and inverse-power (red) functions. (b) Dependence of the local detuning function fi(a) on the control parameter a. The green curves correspond to the exponential profile fi(a) = exp(−dia), while red represent the power-law profile fi(a) = (1 + di)−a, evaluated for di = 0, . . . , 5. (c) Average infidelity ⟨1 −PMIS⟩for tf · Ωmax = 25π for a graph ensemble, comparing six scaling functions, fi(a).

Fig. 5. Statistical analysis of the data presented in Fig.3. (a) Probability density of the logarithmic error ratio, log10[(1 − Ptrad)/(1 −PLD)]. (b) Probability density of the hardness parameter ratio, HPtrad/HPLD.
